title: On apology as labor
summary: Argue that apology, especially in service work, is a form of unpaid emotional labor — and consider what acknowledging that would change.
prompt: Write a 600–800-word argumentative essay claiming that apology in many contemporary workplaces — call centres, hotels, schools, hospitals — has hardened into a form of work, often unpaid and unequally distributed. Use concrete examples from at least two countries (e.g. Manila, Berlin, Tokyo, New York). Use one inversion, one cleft, and complex concession. Avoid moralising and TED openings.
hintStarters: There is a temptation to think of apology as a moment, not a shift…
What is striking is how rarely the apologiser is the person at fault…
Granted, no employee is forced at gunpoint to say sorry…
Only by reframing apology as labour does the…

hintVocab.meaningVi: sự nhường nhịn, tỏ ra kính trọng

sampleText:
There is a temptation to think of apology as a moment, not a shift. Someone is wronged; someone else says, with whatever sincerity is available, that they are sorry; the matter closes. That picture, comforting as it is, has not described most service apologies for at least a generation. In a call centre in Pasig, in a hotel lobby in Berlin, in a hospital reception in São Paulo, what is happening when an employee apologises is closer to scheduled work than to a private moral act. It is timed, scripted, audited and, in many cases, scored.

What is striking is how rarely the apologiser is the person at fault. The young woman in Manila who tells a furious customer in Sydney that she is 'very sorry for the inconvenience' did not write the refund policy, did not lose the parcel, and has no authority to fix the underlying mistake. Yet it is her voice that absorbs the anger, her shift report that records the de-escalation, her tone that will be reviewed for compliance with the company's service charter. The apology has been outsourced — not merely geographically, but morally. The institution that made the error has handed the emotional cost of that error to the person least responsible for it, and then metricised her performance of carrying it.

Once one sees this, the word 'sorry' itself begins to look different. Many of the apologies one hears in commercial life are agentless by design — 'we apologise for any inconvenience caused' — built to acknowledge harm while withholding the subject who caused it. The grammar does the work that, in a different register, lawyers would have to do. Sorry becomes a soft form of denial; the harm is named, the agent disappears, and the listener is expected to accept the trade. It is precisely this grammatical sleight of hand that explains why so many corporate apologies feel, even when carefully delivered, like nothing has been admitted.

Granted, no employee is forced at gunpoint to say sorry, and apologies, even scripted ones, can still defuse a tense moment and protect a tired person on the other side of the counter. Even so, treating apology as labour rather than as feeling changes what we can ask of it. We can ask whether it is compensable. We can ask whether it is asymmetrically and often gendered — front-of-house apology work in hotels in Tokyo, in nursing wards in Dublin, in airline ground crews almost anywhere, falls overwhelmingly on women. We can ask whether the apologiser is being paid for the absorption itself, or whether the institution is, quietly, banking on her absorbing it for free.

Only by reframing apology as labour does the demand for redress sharpen. A genuine apology, in the old sense, was an offer of moral repair, often costly to the one offering it. A scripted apology, repeated forty times an hour, performed under the threat of a poor performance review and a cancelled shift differential, is something else: a piece of frontline absorption work, valuable to the firm and invisible to the customer. To call it work is not to make it less sincere; many frontline workers mean what they say. It is to make visible the price of saying it.

What would change if we treated apology this way? Modestly, perhaps, the shift report would record not just the apology but the cost of producing it. Service-level agreements would mention emotional load alongside response time. Training would acknowledge, without embarrassment, that the script is doing the company's repair work — and that the body performing the script should not also be expected to subsidise it. None of this is utopia; it is bookkeeping. But it is bookkeeping of a kind that the language of 'we apologise for any inconvenience' has been built, rather skilfully, to prevent. That, one might argue, is the clearest indication that it is overdue.

được chấm điểm

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Inversion after 'Only by …'
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'Only by reframing apology as labour does the demand for redress sharpen.' đảo trợ động từ 'does' lên trước chủ ngữ 'the demand'. Sau cụm 'Only by + V-ing', mệnh đề chính bắt buộc phải đảo ngữ.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: The sentence 'Only by reframing apology as labour does the demand for redress sharpen.' inverts the auxiliary 'does' before the subject 'the demand'. After 'Only by + V-ing', the main clause must invert.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Cleft for rhetorical focus
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Cấu trúc 'It is precisely this grammatical sleight of hand that explains why…' là một câu chẻ (cleft) — đẩy cụm danh từ then chốt lên đầu để nhấn mạnh tác nhân của hiệu ứng đang được phân tích.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: The structure 'It is precisely this grammatical sleight of hand that explains why…' is a cleft — fronting the key noun phrase to spotlight the cause of the effect under analysis.

sampleTranslationVi:
Có một khuynh hướng nghĩ rằng lời xin lỗi là một khoảnh khắc, chứ không phải một ca làm. Có người bị làm sai; người khác nói, với mức độ chân thành có thể có, rằng họ xin lỗi; sự việc khép lại. Bức tranh đó, dù dễ chịu, đã không còn mô tả phần lớn các lời xin lỗi dịch vụ từ ít nhất một thế hệ nay. Trong một tổng đài ở Pasig, ở sảnh khách sạn Berlin, ở quầy tiếp nhận của một bệnh viện São Paulo, điều xảy ra khi một nhân viên xin lỗi gần với công việc được sắp lịch hơn là một hành vi đạo đức riêng tư. Nó được tính giờ, viết kịch bản, ghi âm và, trong nhiều trường hợp, chấm điểm.

Điều đáng chú ý là người xin lỗi rất hiếm khi là người có lỗi. Cô gái trẻ ở Manila nói với khách hàng đang giận ở Sydney rằng cô 'rất xin lỗi vì sự bất tiện' không viết chính sách hoàn tiền, không làm mất kiện hàng, và không có quyền sửa lỗi gốc. Tuy vậy, chính giọng cô hứng cơn giận, báo cáo ca của cô ghi lại cuộc hạ nhiệt, tông giọng của cô sẽ bị xem xét theo tiêu chuẩn dịch vụ của công ty. Lời xin lỗi đã được thuê ngoài — không chỉ về mặt địa lí, mà cả về mặt đạo đức.

Khi đã thấy điều đó, chữ 'xin lỗi' tự nó bắt đầu trông khác. Nhiều lời xin lỗi trong đời sống thương mại được thiết kế không có chủ thể — 'chúng tôi xin lỗi vì mọi bất tiện đã gây ra' — được dựng lên để công nhận tổn hại trong khi giấu đi kẻ gây ra. Ngữ pháp làm công việc mà, ở một thanh ngữ khác, luật sư sẽ phải làm.

Cứ cho là không nhân viên nào bị ép bằng súng phải nói xin lỗi, và những lời xin lỗi, dù có kịch bản, vẫn có thể làm dịu một khoảnh khắc căng thẳng. Dù vậy, đối xử với lời xin lỗi như lao động, không phải như cảm xúc, thay đổi điều ta có thể hỏi về nó. Ta có thể hỏi nó có nên được trả công không. Ta có thể hỏi liệu nó có bất đối xứng và mang tính giới không — công việc xin lỗi ở khách sạn Tokyo, ở khoa điều dưỡng ở Dublin, ở tiếp viên hàng không hầu như khắp nơi, gần như toàn bộ rơi vào phụ nữ.

Chỉ bằng cách đặt lại lời xin lỗi như lao động, yêu cầu đền bù mới trở nên sắc nét. Một lời xin lỗi đích thực, theo nghĩa cũ, là một lời đề nghị sửa chữa đạo đức, thường tốn kém cho người đưa ra. Một lời xin lỗi có kịch bản, lặp lại bốn mươi lần một giờ, dưới đe doạ điểm hiệu suất xấu và một ca bị huỷ, là một thứ khác: công việc hấp thụ ở tuyến đầu, có giá trị với doanh nghiệp và vô hình với khách hàng.

Điều gì sẽ thay đổi nếu ta đối xử với lời xin lỗi như vậy? Có lẽ khiêm tốn thôi, báo cáo ca sẽ ghi không chỉ lời xin lỗi mà cả chi phí tạo ra nó. Các thoả thuận mức dịch vụ sẽ đề cập tải trọng cảm xúc bên cạnh thời gian phản hồi. Đó, người ta có thể lập luận, là dấu hiệu rõ nhất rằng việc này đã quá hạn.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence demonstrates inversion correctly after a restrictive opener?
options: Only by reframing apology as labour the demand for redress sharpens.
Only the demand for redress sharpens by reframing apology.
Only by reframing apology as labour, sharpens the demand.
Only by reframing apology as labour does the demand for redress sharpen.
mcQuestions.explanation: After 'Only by + V-ing', the main clause inverts: auxiliary 'does' precedes the subject.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option is the closest C1-register paraphrase of 'we apologise for any inconvenience caused'?
options: An agentless apology that names harm while concealing who caused it.
A very sincere expression of personal regret.
An admission of guilt and an offer of compensation.
A statement that someone was definitely at fault.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay specifically calls these apologies 'agentless by design' — naming harm but withholding the subject.
mcQuestions.hint: The phrase hides the actor; pick the option that names that move.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition fits the concession-then-counter move best?
options: Therefore no employee is forced … therefore treating apology changes things.
Granted, no employee is forced at gunpoint to say sorry … Even so, treating apology as labour rather than as feeling changes what we can ask of it.
Furthermore, no one is forced … on the bright side, things change.
In addition, no employee is forced … in conclusion, things change.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted … Even so …' is the canonical concession-and-counter pair.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for concede first, then turn.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a cleft most effectively?
options: Precisely this grammatical sleight of hand, it explains the feeling.
Explains this grammatical sleight of hand the corporate apology feels.
It is precisely this grammatical sleight of hand that explains why so many corporate apologies feel like nothing has been admitted.
Corporate apologies feel like nothing because grammar admits.
mcQuestions.explanation: A is a classic 'It is X that …' cleft, focusing on the causal mechanism.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'It is X that …' framing.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which item best matches the register of the essay?
options: Saying sorry is, like, basically a job that nobody pays you for.
People at work say sorry all the time, which is interesting.
Sorry is a word that people use in a lot of customer situations.
Apology in many service settings has hardened into a form of work, often unpaid and unequally distributed.
mcQuestions.explanation: A nominalises ('a form of work'), uses precise modifiers, and matches the essay's controlled tone.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for nominalization and precise modifiers.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses cohesion most carefully?
options: Sorry becomes a soft form of denial; the harm is named, the agent disappears, and the listener is expected to accept the trade.
Sorry is denial. Harm. Agent gone. Listener accepts.
Sorry, denial, harm, agent, listener, trade — all together.
Soft denial because sorry is harm with no agent and listener trade.
mcQuestions.explanation: A uses a semicolon and three parallel clauses to layer the move.

options: In conclusion, apologies are very important things in life.
That, one might argue, is the clearest indication that it is overdue.
Throughout history, people have always said sorry.
It is important to note that apologies have value.
mcQuestions.explanation: A hedges and lands; the others use banned filler openers.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid 'In conclusion', 'Throughout history', 'It is important to note'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence is closest in topic and register to the essay?
options: Companies are mean to their workers, which is bad.
Errors happen and someone has to deal with them.
The institution that made the error has handed the emotional cost of that error to the person least responsible for it.
Apologies in workplaces are a thing that happens often.
mcQuestions.explanation: A is precise about who passes the cost to whom — matching the essay's core claim.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the sentence that names agent and recipient of the cost.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which collocation is correct in this context?
options: frontline absorbing job-work
front-of-frontline absorbed work
absorbed frontline-style working
frontline absorption work
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Frontline absorption work' is the exact phrase the essay coins to describe the unpaid emotional load.
mcQuestions.hint: Pick the noun-phrase the essay actually uses.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which is the best hedge for a careful claim about apology?
options: one might argue
obviously, beyond any doubt
as everyone definitely knows
in actual fact, certainly
mcQuestions.explanation: 'one might argue' matches the essay's deliberately cautious register.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence demonstrates an effective parenthetical aside?
options: The apology was geographically and morally outsourced together.
The apology has been outsourced — not merely geographically, but morally.
Outsourced geographically and morally was the apology.
Apology, outsourced and moral, both happen geographically.
mcQuestions.explanation: A uses an em-dash to gloss the kind of outsourcing meant.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the em-dashed clarifying clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence keeps cohesion best when widening the example?
options: Apology work hotels, wards, crews — women in many places.
Tokyo Dublin and other places, women do apology work.
front-of-house apology work in hotels in Tokyo, in nursing wards in Dublin, in airline ground crews almost anywhere, falls overwhelmingly on women.
Many places have apology work that is done by women, mostly.
mcQuestions.explanation: A uses parallel prepositional phrases to give the example rhetorical weight.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for repeated 'in …' phrases with parallel structure.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option is closest to: 'the institution is, quietly, banking on her absorbing it for free'?
options: The institution loudly pays her for the absorption.
The bank is quiet about her free absorption.
She quietly banks money from the institution.
The institution is silently relying on her to take the cost without payment.
mcQuestions.explanation: A keeps the agent ('institution') and the move ('relying on her without paying').
mcQuestions.hint: 'Banking on' means relying on, not literal banking.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence pattern matches the essay's defining move?
options: Apology is X, not Y — and the difference matters.
Apology is a thing that people do sometimes when it is needed.
There are apologies of various kinds in many places at all times.
Apology and labour and work and sorry are all words.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay repeatedly uses an 'X, not Y' frame to redefine apology.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which counter-argument would the essay most need to address?
options: Apologies are unnecessary in service work.
A scripted apology can still be sincere and useful — does treating it as labour deny that?
Customers always deserve to be apologised to.
Apologies should always be free of charge.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay acknowledges sincerity but reframes the question to cost — A is the most serious objection.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the objection the essay itself anticipates.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Recall the last time someone apologised to you on behalf of a company. Who was actually at fault, who said sorry, and who paid the emotional cost? What, if anything, would have changed if that cost had appeared on a receipt?
